title: Arrival Checklist — Essential Tasks Upon Arrival
body:
## Arrival Checklist for New ESL Teachers in Korea

Welcome to South Korea! The first few weeks can be overwhelming, so here is a prioritized checklist to help you settle in smoothly.

### Day 1-3: Immediate Priorities

- [ ] **Activate your Korean phone** — Purchase a prepaid SIM at the airport (T-Mobile, KT, or SK Telecom booths are in the arrivals hall). You can upgrade to a full plan once you have your ARC.
- [ ] **Get transportation**: Purchase a **T-money card** at any convenience store (GS25, CU, 7-Eleven). Load it with 20,000-30,000 KRW to start.
- [ ] **Exchange currency** — Airport exchange rates are fair. ATMs at airports accept international cards.
- [ ] **Meet your employer** — Confirm your school address, first workday, and any training schedule.
- [ ] **Check your housing** — Document the condition of your apartment with photos and video (important for deposit return).

### Week 1: Medical & Registration

- [ ] **Medical examination** — Your employer should arrange this at a designated hospital. The exam includes blood tests, urinalysis, chest X-ray, and drug test. Cost: approximately 90,000-100,000 KRW (often covered by employer).
- [ ] **Alien Registration Card (ARC)** — Visit your local immigration office with: passport, employment contract, passport photos, medical results, and application form. Processing takes about 2-3 weeks.

### Week 2-4: Getting Established

- [ ] **Bank account** — Open a Korean bank account (KEB Hana, Shinhan, and Woori are foreigner-friendly). You will need your ARC, passport, and employment contract.
- [ ] **National Health Insurance (NHIS)** — Your employer will enroll you. Coverage begins after your ARC is issued.
- [ ] **Phone plan upgrade** — With your ARC and bank account, sign up for a monthly plan.
- [ ] **Register your address** — Your employer typically handles this, but confirm it has been done.

### Useful Apps to Download

- **Naver Map** or **Kakao Map** — essential for navigation (Google Maps has limited functionality in Korea)
- **KakaoTalk** — Korea's primary messaging app (your school will likely use it)
- **Papago** — Naver's translation app (Korean-English, excellent accuracy)
- **Coupang** — Online shopping with next-day delivery

### Emergency Numbers

- **Police**: 112
- **Fire/Ambulance**: 119
- **Immigration Contact Center**: 1345 (English available)

title: Visa Change — How to Switch to an E Visa
body:
## How to Switch to an E-2 Visa from Another Visa Type

If you are currently in Korea on a different visa (tourist, D-10 job-seeking, etc.) and have secured a teaching position, you may be able to switch to an E-2 visa without leaving the country.

### Eligible Visa Types for In-Country Change

You can generally apply for an in-country visa status change from:

- **B-1/B-2 (Tourist/Transit)** — limited cases; immigration may require you to leave and re-enter
- **D-10 (Job Seeking)** — the most common and straightforward conversion
- **D-2 (Student)** — after completing your studies
- **E-2 Transfer** — changing employers (see the E-2 Transfer post for details)
- **H-1 (Working Holiday)** — possible but varies by immigration office

### Required Documents

1. **Passport** and current **ARC** (if applicable)
2. **Visa Status Change Application Form** (통합신청서)
3. **Employment contract** signed by both you and the employer
4. **Employer's business registration certificate** (사업자등록증)
5. **Apostilled degree** (if not already submitted to Korean immigration)
6. **Apostilled criminal background check** (may be waived if already on file and you have maintained continuous residence)
7. **Passport photos** (3.5x4.5cm)
8. **Application fee**: 130,000 KRW

### Process

1. **Book an appointment** at your local immigration office via Hi Korea (hikorea.go.kr)
2. Gather all documents — check with your immigration office for any additional requirements
3. **Submit your application** at the immigration office
4. Processing time: **2-4 weeks** (you may receive a temporary sticker in your passport during this period)
5. If approved, your ARC will be updated or a new one issued

### Important Considerations

- **D-10 holders**: You must have registered your job-seeking activities and your D-10 must still be valid
- **Tourist visa holders**: In-country changes are becoming stricter. Some immigration offices may require you to exit Korea and apply from abroad
- **Timing**: You cannot work at the new school until the visa change is approved
- Always confirm current requirements with your local immigration office, as policies can change

title: Housing Guide — Accommodation for Teachers
body:
## Housing Guide for ESL Teachers in Korea

Understanding Korean housing is important before you arrive. Here is what to expect and how the system works.

### Employer-Provided Housing

Most hagwon (private academy) positions include **free housing** as part of the compensation package:

- Typically a **furnished studio apartment** (원룸) near the school
- Basic furnishings: bed, desk, wardrobe, washing machine, refrigerator, stove/range, air conditioner
- Utilities (electricity, gas, water, internet) are usually the teacher's responsibility
- The apartment is provided **for the duration of your contract** only

### Housing Allowance

Some employers offer a **housing allowance** instead of a provided apartment:

- Typically **300,000-500,000 KRW per month** in addition to your salary
- You are responsible for finding your own accommodation
- This option gives you more freedom but requires more effort and Korean language skills

### Understanding Korean Rental Systems

**Jeonse (전세)** — Large lump-sum deposit, no monthly rent:
- Deposit: 50-80% of the property's value
- Not practical for most foreign teachers

**Wolse (월세)** — Monthly rent with smaller deposit:
- Deposit (보증금): 3,000,000-10,000,000 KRW
- Monthly rent: 400,000-800,000 KRW (varies greatly by location)
- Most common option for foreigners renting independently

**Goshiwon / Officetel**:
- Goshiwon: Small furnished rooms, 300,000-500,000 KRW/month, minimal deposit
- Officetel: Studio apartments in commercial buildings, higher quality

### Tips for Finding Housing

- **Zigbang** and **Dabang** apps are Korea's main apartment listing platforms
- **Real estate agents** (부동산) are on nearly every block — they charge 0.3-0.5% of the transaction as commission
- Always get a **contract in writing** and have someone who reads Korean review it
- **Take photos** of everything when you move in — document any existing damage
- Check for **mold** (common in older buildings), heating system functionality, and water pressure

### Average Monthly Costs (Utilities)

| Item | Cost (KRW) |
|------|------------|
| Electricity | 30,000-80,000 |
| Gas (heating + cooking) | 20,000-100,000 (seasonal) |
| Water | 10,000-20,000 |
| Internet | 20,000-30,000 |
| **Total** | **80,000-230,000** |

title: Work Certificate — Experience Certificate Guide
body:
## How to Get a Work Experience Certificate in Korea

A **work experience certificate** (경력증명서) is an important document that verifies your employment history. You will need it for future job applications, visa transfers, and professional references.

### What Is an Experience Certificate?

An experience certificate is an official document from your employer confirming:

- Your name and personal details
- The name and address of the employer
- Your job title and responsibilities
- Dates of employment (start and end)
- Sometimes: salary information and reason for leaving

This is **different** from a personal reference letter. An experience certificate is a formal HR document, while a reference letter is a personal endorsement.

### When Do You Need It?

- **Changing jobs in Korea** — New employers and immigration may request proof of previous employment
- **Visa transfer** — Immigration offices sometimes require employment verification
- **Future employment abroad** — Demonstrates your teaching experience
- **EPIK/GEPIK applications** — Government teaching programs require documented experience
- **Salary negotiation** — Verified experience can justify higher pay

### How to Request One

1. **Ask your employer/HR department** directly — they are legally obligated to provide one under Korean Labor Standards Act (Article 39)
2. Request it **before your last day** when possible
3. Ask for both **Korean and English** versions
4. Make sure it includes the **company stamp** (직인) — without it, the document may not be recognized
5. Request **multiple originals** if possible

### What If Your Employer Refuses?

Under Korean law, employers **must** issue an experience certificate upon request. If they refuse:

1. Make the request in **writing** (email or KakaoTalk message for a record)
2. Contact the **Ministry of Employment and Labor** complaint hotline: **1350**
3. File a complaint with your local **Labor Board** (노동위원회)
4. BRIDGE can assist in mediating — contact us

### Template Information

A proper experience certificate should contain:

- Company letterhead
- Certificate title (경력증명서 / Certificate of Employment)
- Employee information (name, date of birth, nationality)
- Employment period
- Position and duties
- Date of issuance
- Company name, representative name, and **official stamp**

title: ARC Renewal — Updating ARC for New Employment
body:
## Updating Your ARC for New Employment

When you change employers or extend your stay in Korea, you need to update your **Alien Registration Card (ARC)**. Here is how the process works.

### When to Update Your ARC

- **New employer**: When transferring your E-2 visa to a new school
- **Contract extension**: When renewing your contract with the same employer
- **Address change**: When moving to a new residence
- **Passport renewal**: When you get a new passport
- **Visa extension**: When extending your period of stay

### Documents Required (for employer change)

1. **Current ARC** (original)
2. **Passport** (original)
3. **New employment contract** — signed by both parties
4. **New employer's business registration certificate** (사업자등록증)
5. **Letter of Release** from previous employer (고용변동 확인서)
6. **Application form** (통합신청서)
7. **Passport photo** (3.5x4.5cm) — 1 copy
8. **Fee**: 30,000 KRW (for ARC reissuance)

### Process

1. **Book an appointment** on Hi Korea (hikorea.go.kr) — walk-ins may face long waits
2. Visit your **local immigration office** during business hours (9:00-18:00, closed weekends)
3. Submit all documents at the designated counter
4. You will receive a **receipt** — keep this as proof of your pending application
5. Your updated ARC will be mailed to your registered address in **2-3 weeks**
6. The receipt serves as a temporary ARC during the processing period

### Important Deadlines

- **Employer change**: Report within **15 days** of starting at the new workplace
- **Address change**: Report within **15 days** of moving
- **Visa extension**: Apply **at least 4 weeks before** your current visa expires
- Late reporting may result in fines (100,000-200,000 KRW)

### Online Reporting Options

Some changes can be reported online through Hi Korea:

- **Address change**: Can often be done online without visiting immigration
- **Visa extension**: Some visa types allow online extension applications
- **Status check**: Track your application progress online with your receipt number

### Tips

- Always carry your ARC or the receipt with you — Korean law requires foreigners to carry identification
- If you lose your ARC, report it immediately to immigration and apply for a replacement (fee: 30,000 KRW)
- Keep a photocopy or photo of your ARC on your phone as backup
- Your ARC number is your key identifier for banking, phone contracts, and hospital visits in Korea
